title: Silence, trimmed

items.text: New Silence trim section: cuts lead-in and run-out silence during conversion. Surco detects it and suggests the cut on the waveform, and you fine-tune the exact edges with two handles — with zoom and arrow-key nudging.

title: Clicks under control

items.text: Click repair now estimates how many audible clicks a track carries before you convert.
Three repair intensities — Gentle, Standard and Strong — in a single control, next to the hear-what-gets-removed audition.

title: Hear the repair, read the editor at a glance

items.text: Click repair can now be auditioned before converting: it plays 20 seconds from the middle of the track with only the noise it would remove — near-silence means the music stays untouched — along with the share of the excerpt it would touch.
Folded editor sections now summarize their state: artist and title, the file name that will be written, the normalization targets or a plain “Off” — and their buttons fold with them.
Editor sections can now be hidden entirely from Settings → Editor, and click repair starts folded.

title: See each channel, trust the peaks

items.text: New per-channel view: split the editor waveform into stacked left and right lanes with a toggle.
Clipping marks now flag real full-scale clipping, measured sample by sample on the original audio, like Audacity.
Search waits for the file's tags to load before firing, instead of guessing from the file name.
When the release doesn't show up, search retries without the DJ prefix or with just the lead act before falling back to the bare title.
New vinyl click repair: an opt-in stage that detects stylus clicks and pops and repairs them during conversion, with two strengths and a count of repaired clicks.
Normalization no longer fails when the loudness target or peak ceiling was typed out of range — they are clamped to the values ffmpeg accepts.
Strong click repair no longer stalls the conversion: it runs at full speed with the same effect on long pops.
On Windows, the track list and search read the file name instead of the full path.

title: Explore the wave and make the editor yours

items.text: The loudness section shows the track's waveform from the start, with clipping peaks marked and a preview of how it will look after normalizing.
Explore the wave up close: a readout under the cursor, rekordbox-style stepped zoom with horizontal scroll, and a toggle that flags the peaks that would clip with the current setting.
The editor sections are yours: drag to reorder them and pick which start folded, from Settings → Editor.
The Discogs panel jumps straight to the matched release, without reselecting the track.
Search finds the release even when the title drags trailing rip stamps or “presenta” credits.
The convert button mirrors the conversion's stages, just like the track row.

title: See what normalization really does

items.text: New before/after comparison when converting: the source and converted waveforms, with each file's real loudness and peak figures.
Two ways to compare: side by side or overlaid, with a slider to fade one wave over the other and spot the difference.
Peak normalization can now remove DC offset and normalize each stereo channel independently, like in Audacity.
Change the normalization after exporting and the button offers “Update” again — no need to touch a tag.
Words to ignore in searches now also apply when re-running a search you had already made.

title: You decide where every conversion lands

items.text: New “Next to the original” destination: the conversion is written beside the source file without ever touching it. Same-format exports take a “(2)” name instead of overwriting anything.
The convert button now also lets you pick the destination for just that conversion — folder, Apple Music, Engine DJ, next to the original or overwrite — without changing your Settings.
New FLAC option on macOS: show covers in Finder and Quick Look, which normally don't read FLAC artwork.
After an export, picking another format from the menu now shows on the button — “Re-export (FLAC)” — so you always know what the next click will write.

Clearer Settings: the output folder and the Engine DJ fields live under their destination choice (in the welcome wizard too, which now lets you change the folder), details unfold with an animation, and switching normalization on scrolls its fields into view.
Fixed the “Maximum results” setting: the selector rendered clipped and forced a horizontal scrollbar onto the Search tab.

title: Search that understands your titles

items.text: If you use a title format like “(A2) Title (1998)”, matching now recognizes it: your own pattern is undone when searching and scoring, so a renamed track finds its record on the first try.
New Search setting: words to ignore (defaults to “vinyl, rip”). Rip signatures and watermarks in titles are skipped when searching and matching — your tags are never changed.
A track that carries its saved Discogs release now auto-matches directly when the title fits, including records that list no durations.
The list filter can now split matched tracks by their source: Discogs or Bandcamp.

title: Sharper matches, in your own language

items.text: Auto-match now tries the release ID already stored on the file before falling back to a title search, landing on the right Discogs release more often.
The app's native menu and dialogs now follow the language you pinned in Settings, instead of falling back to your system's locale.
Fixed a crash on launch for Intel Macs.

A file that already carries its Discogs release now opens straight to that exact disc — auto-match and the search panel stick to it instead of burying it in title-search results, and only fall back to a search when the stored release no longer fits.

title: Title patterns and an organized palette

items.text: Configurable title format, collectors' style: set a pattern like ({trackNumber}) {title} ({year}) in Settings → Naming and apply it when you choose — to one track from the title field's ⋯ menu, or to the whole selection at once with the editor's T button or the command palette. It never runs on its own, and ⌘Z undoes it.
The Naming tab, redesigned: the title format comes first, and both formats carry the editor fields' ⋯ menu for inserting {fields}, sorted alphabetically with a live preview.
The command palette (⌘K) groups actions into sections — Metadata, Convert & export, Library… — and search now ignores accents: “titulo” finds “título”.
Applying the title format twice no longer doubles the prefix, and the app always explains the outcome: how many titles changed, which were skipped and which empty field was in the way.

title: Tags that read clean in every editor

items.text: Conversions now clean up the tag variants other editors leave behind (LABEL, ORGANIZATION, ALBUMARTIST2…): no more duplicated label or album artist, and emptying a field now truly removes it from the file.
WAV files are tagged as ID3v2.3, the version Mp3tag and friends read — and converted files no longer carry the encoder's ENCODER stamp.
New artwork option: upscale smaller covers to your configured size. Combined with the square crop, your whole library comes out with identical covers (say, 700×700).
The ⋯ menu for borrowing another field's value or fixing text case is now on every free-text field: mix name, remixer, composer, label and catalog number included.

title: Crash-safe edits and faster conversions

items.text: Your unapplied edits no longer get lost: they are saved automatically as you work, and if the app closes unexpectedly you can reopen the session with everything just as you left it — metadata, covers and matches included. When there is work at stake, the reopen prompt waits for your answer instead of fading away on its own.
Bulk converting is noticeably faster: conversions run in parallel across your Mac's cores, converting to the same format is nearly instant, and each track needs fewer passes.
The activity panel explains every auto-match verdict: which signals matched, which didn't, and which candidates were probed.

title: Google search and shareable stats

items.text: New editor button to search Google for a track: it opens your browser with the file name already searched, no copy-paste needed.
Share your stats: the Statistics tab now generates an image with your lifetime numbers, ready to show off on social media.
Double-A-side vinyl positions (AA, AA1) are also kept as the track number when applying a Discogs release.

title: Your stats, and faster batch editing

items.text: The Stats tab now counts all your activity: tracks loaded, played, analyzed and found on Discogs or Bandcamp, next to a progress bar toward your next conversion milestone. All visible at a glance, no scrolling.
Copying and pasting metadata between tracks now includes the cover too, and ⌘Z undoes the whole thing.
New command to number the selected tracks in list order (1, 2, 3…), made for albums that are not on Discogs.

title: Conversion quality, under your control

items.text: Choose the quality of every conversion: MP3 bitrate (320 down to 128 kbps, or V0/V2 VBR), bit depth, sample rate and FLAC compression level. Everything defaults to matching the original — maximum fidelity without touching a thing.

Conversions keep the source's exact bit depth: a 44.1/16 file no longer comes out 24-bit when normalizing, reducing to 16-bit applies dither, and an MP3 converted to WAV or AIFF yields integer PCM every player accepts.
Conversion settings now live in two tabs: Conversion (format and quality) and Output (folder, Apple Music, Engine DJ).
Send the originals of already-converted tracks to the trash in one action; bulk actions work on your multi-selection when you have one.
Discogs vinyl positions (A1, B2…) are kept as the track number, both in the tags and in {trackNumber} when naming files, instead of being reduced to a plain number.

A file already in the output format still updates without re-encoding — but when it doesn't meet your pinned sample rate or bit depth, the editor now offers an explicit re-encode, leaving the original untouched.
More comfortable Settings: tabs ordered by workflow, editor fields reorder by dragging (with their internal name shown on hover), and the rating can now name your files.

title: Surco in five languages, and matching you can trust

items.text: Surco now speaks German, French and Brazilian Portuguese, alongside Spanish and English. Pick a language in Settings or let it follow your system.

Auto-matching is more careful: an identical title alone no longer tags a file on its own — duration, artist or catalog number has to corroborate it, and otherwise the suggestion is flagged for you to confirm.
Discogs search now finds album tracks: when the title doesn't match a release, it searches inside tracklists; and catalog numbers are looked up on their exact field instead of as free text.
Save a quality report as a PNG from the editor: the spectrogram with its verdict and cutoff line, ready to share whenever someone asks whether a file is really lossless.
First-run setup is down to four steps; file naming, genre shortcuts and editor fields wait for you in Settings.
Remove the selected track with Backspace or Delete; the selection hops to its neighbour.

title: Replace your old Apple Music copy

items.text: When a converted track lands in Apple Music, Surco spots the old copy of the same song and offers to remove it: it shows you exactly which one, removes the library entry and sends its file to the Trash, where you can recover it.
Batch conversions can be cancelled: while one runs you'll see its progress in the toolbar, and one click there (or the command in the palette) stops it.
Serato exports saved to a USB drive now locate the files on import.
Re-converting a track Engine DJ already analyzed keeps its analysis: length and BPM are no longer lost.
Search results are ranked by relevance, with your preferred provider as the tie-break.
Surco checks for updates now and then while it stays open, not just at launch.
Dismissing the “reopen your last session” offer — with the ✕ or letting it expire — now counts as an answer: it won't come back on the next launch.

title: A clearer export finish

items.text: When an export finishes, the editor gets to the point: the confirmation with links to show the file or delete the original, and a single row of buttons — Apple Music, another DJ app and re-export.
The welcome wizard offers Engine DJ and the destination choice on every platform, not just macOS.
The “reopen your last session” prompt now dismisses itself after a few seconds, with a visible countdown — and every notice now eases out gently instead of vanishing abruptly.

title: Quieter prompts

items.text: The prompt for new tracks detected in your folder now dismisses itself after a few seconds, with a visible countdown — and once you decline it, it won't offer you the same files again.

title: Straight to Engine DJ (Denon)

items.text: New Engine DJ conversion destination: every converted track is registered straight into your Engine DJ (Denon) library, with its cover, rating and a configurable playlist — open Engine and they're there.
If Engine DJ is open when you convert, Surco offers to close it and carries on — it never writes to the library while Engine is using it.
The “already in your library” badge now follows your destination: it checks Apple Music or Engine DJ depending on where your tracks go, and hides when you only convert to a folder.
Exporting to rekordbox, Traktor, Serato or M3U8 now lives in the editor, right where a conversion finishes, instead of a lone toolbar button.
After an update, Surco shows you what's new the next time it opens.
Removing tracks from the list no longer makes Surco offer to load them back as if they were new.

title: Undo, sessions that come back and ALAC
items: Undo with ⌘Z: if a batch fill, a find & replace or a clear tramples metadata it shouldn't have, one shortcut puts everything back the way it was.
Surco remembers your last session: on launch, a prompt offers to reload the tracks you were working on.
Automatic quality analysis (optional): turn it on in Settings and every imported track is analyzed in the background, so suspect files flag themselves without pressing Analyze.
New ALAC (Apple Lossless) output format — lossless and ready for Apple Music; and for MP3 you can now choose between 320 kbps and V0.
Duplicates filter: find the same song loaded as two files — a FLAC and an MP3, two downloads — and decide which one to keep.
Export your list as M3U8, the standard playlist almost any player understands.
More day-to-day clarity: a notice when a file's metadata can't be read, Stats shows the current list's progress, and the activity panel remembers its position and now logs imports and exports too.

title: Sharper matching and clean-up at a glance
items: Auto-matching gets more accurate: when a track carries a catalog number, Surco confirms the exact release with it and applies the match without asking you to review it; and between two editions, it prefers the one whose year matches your file.
When a suggestion lands as "review", you can now accept it in one step — from the palette (⌘K) or its shortcut — without opening the editor.
A button to send every track flagged as suspect to the Trash at once, right beside the quality filter.
The player gains a play button over the cover on hover, another to jump to the track that's playing, and 5-second skip back and forward with the arrow keys.
More keyboard control: search with "/", clear the search and filters with Escape, step between tracks with Ctrl+arrows while editing a field, and every action shows its shortcut in the tooltip.

title: A sectioned editor and a palette that learns
items: The editor groups fields into sections (Identity, Catalog, DJ and Order) so you stay oriented when many are on; in Settings → Fields, an "Auto-organize" button sorts them into sections in one click.
The command palette (⌘K) learns from you: as you type, the commands you use most rise to the top of the list, so your usual one lands under the cursor.
Discogs search matches more precisely: when a track carries an artist and title, it queries those fields separately instead of one loose string, finding the right release with less noise.

title: Parallel conversion and more from ⌘K
items: "Convert all" now processes several tracks at once instead of one after another, so a big batch finishes far sooner; the Apple Music step still runs one at a time, the way Music imports them.
The command palette (⌘K) gains new shortcuts: switch the theme, fill metadata from the file name, clear the selection's metadata, and a direct link to donate (with a little confetti).

title: Faster engine, filters that stick
items: Surco now runs on a newer engine (Electron 42, Chromium 148) — snappier rendering for the waveform and spectrum, and the latest security fixes under the hood.

The track list's toolbar actions — Find & Replace, Auto-Match, Analyze quality, Fill tags and Empty — act on the visible tracks, never the ones a filter is hiding; while "Clear the list" from ⌘K still wipes everything for a clean start.

title: Tidier toolbar, richer Discogs results
items: The actions that work on your track list — add files, select all, fill tags, find & replace, clear — now live in the list's own header instead of the top toolbar, so it's clear what each one touches. The toolbar keeps only the crate-wide tools.
Discogs results are now cards that match your track rows, and each shows more of the release: year, label, catalogue number and format — so it's easier to pick the exact pressing among many.
Filtering the list and then converting now only touches the tracks you can see: filter to MP3 and hit convert, and your FLACs stay untouched.
Select All and Fill tags from filename now act on the visible or selected tracks, never silently on the whole crate.
Find & Replace shows the / / around the field in regex mode, so a pattern like Shake just works instead of being searched literally.
Tracks that Surco auto-loads from a watched folder now play straight away.

title: A redesigned player and cleaner everywhere
items: The playing track's card has been rebuilt: the title now gets its own line so it stops getting cut off, the artwork and controls sit balanced beside it, and the buttons are grouped by what they do — play, loop and waveform, close.

The track list and the player now always show the file's own cover, never a different image you're trying out in the editor — and applying a Discogs release only swaps your artwork when you've asked it to.
The Discogs search box gains a magnifier and a one-click clear, matching the track filter.
Tooltips across the app now match Surco's dark theme instead of the plain system ones, and the toolbar reads calmer with lighter icon buttons.
The audio quality spectrum for the track you've selected now draws right away, even while a whole-crate match is running in the background.

title: Load new downloads without dragging the folder again
items: Surco watches the folders you've already loaded: when new tracks show up (a download finishing, say), it offers to load just the new ones in a click — no need to drag the whole folder in again.
The player gains a real volume slider, more precise than scrolling the wheel over the card.
Notifications are unified into one tidy stack in the bottom-right, with a discreet bar showing how long until each one clears.
Each track's full title now shows in a themed tooltip when you hover its name.

title: Follow background work in the Activity panel
items: A new movable, resizable Activity panel shows background work as it happens: track analysis, Apple Music library checks and links to the published releases. Open it from the toolbar.
The spectrogram gains a crosshair that reads the exact frequency under your cursor as you move across it.
The quality verdict reports the real ceiling of high-rate audio instead of stopping at Nyquist, and sharpens Apple Music library matching further.

title: Catch more fakes and sharpen your Apple Music library
items: The quality verdict catches a new kind of fake 320: tracks with a frequency wall hidden behind a few high-frequency spikes that used to pass as good.

Apple Music "already in my library" detection is more accurate: it matches on title, artist and duration, remembers the Discogs-proven verdict, and shows a checking state while it resolves — so the list, the filter and the editor always agree.
Search spreads results across providers, so a Discogs-heavy query no longer buries the Bandcamp ones.
The cover picker no longer shows the same image more than once.
A new settings button empties the analysis cache to recompute spectrograms and verdicts from scratch.

title: Convert without losing your cues or your tags
items: The spectrogram is bigger and truer to Spek: a codec cut reads as dead air and the highs reach up to 22 kHz.
The file picker now accepts folders and expands them just like dropping them in.
Find and replace has a match-case toggle.
Exporting to Apple Music warns you if a track isn't converted yet.
Converting to any format now keeps Traktor's cue points and beatgrid, not just when normalizing the volume.
Conversion now respects metadata fields you've cleared: delete a comment or other value and the original no longer comes back.
New track-row indicators: the quality verdict shows as a severity icon, and already-converted tracks get a blue check badge.
Tracks show up sooner when you drop large or networked folders, and a stalled network file no longer freezes the quality analysis.
More accurate Discogs lookups when a track's name starts with a number.
The interface stays smooth while you analyze the quality of many tracks at once — the analysis no longer competes with the screen or playback.
The quality verdict is better at spotting tracks with a frequency ceiling the codec smeared over.

title: Run the whole flow from the keyboard
items: The whole workflow is now keyboard-driven: jump between the track list, the search results and the editor, move and page through the list, pick a Discogs or Bandcamp release with the arrow keys, and convert-and-move-on to the next track — all without the mouse.
The command palette now jumps straight to any track — start typing its title or artist to find it.
Tidier search results: a configurable maximum, with compilations pushed down and the most-owned pressing floated up, so the right release is easier to spot.
Hiding the player's waveform now keeps the volume, the elapsed and total time, and a seek bar — so you never lose your place.

title: Filter your search results by source
items: When you search Discogs and Bandcamp together, a new filter lets you show All, Discogs or Bandcamp — and the results are tidier: Discogs releases rank first and each source is capped so the list stays short and relevant.
Adding files now shows a live counter and a determinate progress bar as Surco reads each track's metadata, so you can see exactly how far along an import is.
New opt-in setting to apply your file-name pattern automatically, so converted tracks are named without a manual step.
The player gets a toggle to hide the waveform — handy on a small screen — which also skips the waveform analysis to save the work.

Smarter Apple Music library matching: Surco recognises the same track across more artist and title variations — ampersands, dotted acronyms (D.J. vs DJ), leading DJ/Dr./MC handles, spelled-out numbers vs digits, a leading artist before the title and trailing version markers — so fewer of your copies look missing.

Better release matching for tracks tagged “(Original Mix)”: Surco drops that trailing marker so a plain catalog title still scores, and multi-track album matching leaves a file unassigned rather than forcing a wrong cut.

Cleaner provider searches: Surco leads with the main artist before a “presents” alias and ignores a bracketed version note that only repeats the title, so the right Discogs or Bandcamp release surfaces more often.
The editor's in-library badge now lights up when a confident Discogs suggestion matches a track you already have in your Apple Music library.
Tidier list filters: each sidebar section is now its own independent filter, the menu closes as soon as you pick, and Unconverted sits under All.
Sturdier file handling: Surco reads tags from files other apps choke on and repairs malformed WAV headers instead of failing analysis.
Stability: starting a track over no longer leaves the player sounding after its card has closed.

title: Export to Serato and Engine DJ
items: Export your tracks to Serato DJ and Engine DJ (Denon), on top of rekordbox and Traktor: Surco writes the file each app imports.
Auto-match now flags uncertain matches for you to review before trusting them, instead of dropping them, and shows each track's match confidence.
Fake-lossless detection: a heads-up when a FLAC or WAV is really a lossy source repackaged as lossless.
Filter and sort your tracks by format, with a toggle to flip the sort direction.

title: Now you can search Bandcamp too
items: Search Bandcamp alongside Discogs: turn it on in Settings → Search and results from both sources merge into a single list, each tagged by its origin — great for self-released tracks, netlabels and exclusives that aren't on Discogs.
Auto-match is now a global search setting and can fall back to Bandcamp when Discogs comes up empty (with only Bandcamp it needs no token).
Sharper matching for badly named downloads: Surco strips file-name and title noise, pins the right mix, and gives the file's own artist priority.
New “Base title” clean-up in the Album field menu: strip the version or mix from a title in one tap.
Genres that differ only in case (your “Electronic” vs a source's “electronic”) no longer duplicate — a single pill shows, in your casing.
Your existing covers are kept by default: Surco no longer replaces them unless you opt in to swapping out low-resolution ones.

title: Search the exact edition, in your language
items: Filter Discogs searches by format — vinyl, CD, digital… — from the new Search tab in Settings, so you land on the edition you actually want.
Choose the app's language — System, English or Español — and it's remembered between sessions.
Cleaner Discogs results: duplicate editions are merged, and a remix with its tracks reordered still matches the right one.

Sharper Apple Music library detection: it matches on whole artist words and on the base title —so it spots a copy saved under the canonical title—, refreshes when you return to the app, and re-adds a track if its library copy is gone. (macOS)
Surco warns you when it has to skip loudness normalization because it can't measure the track, instead of doing it silently.
Clearer list and editor: Shift-click selects along the visible order, empty required fields are flagged calmly, and the Convert button explains what's missing before it lets you convert.

title: Filter by what's already in your Apple Music
items: See at a glance which tracks you already have in Apple Music: all your tracks are matched against your library, with sidebar filters to show only the ones you're missing (or the ones already there) — so you never re-import a duplicate. (macOS)
Double-click a track to play it, and double-click again to stop.
The list filters are now a single dropdown that never runs out of room, and the position counter — which track you're on and how many are left — stays visible as you switch filters.

title: Sharper quality verdict and smoother track work
items: The quality verdict has a keener eye: a genuine but dull master is no longer flagged “Review” (good when there's no codec cut), and fake hi-res is now caught — files that claim 48/96 kHz but were upsampled from 44.1 kHz.
Auto-match now sweeps all your tracks, prioritizing the tracks on screen, and stops the moment you turn it off.
Editor sections you collapse stay collapsed as you move between tracks, and aren't analyzed until you open them — quicker when reviewing lots of tracks.
Drag several selected tracks out of Surco at once.
Remove from the list or move to Trash every selected track at once from the right-click menu.
Drag an image straight from your browser as the cover.
The toolbar is reorganized into groups (edit · analyze · convert and export) so every action is easy to find.
The cover viewer steps through every image on the track: the one embedded in the file and the ones from the Discogs release.
Surco starts up faster and scrolls more smoothly through lots of tracks, and the first play sounds instantly.

title: Waveform player and sharper Discogs matching
items: The player now shows the track's waveform: click or drag on it to scrub, with the time floating on top.
Continuous playback: when a track ends, the next one in the list plays. Toggle it with the ∞ button in the player.
Much sharper Discogs matching: it ignores file-name noise (320, labels, “feat.”…), is accent-insensitive, understands more name formats, and retries by catalog number or title alone when needed.
With auto-match on, the track you select is matched on its own — no need to press the button on each one.
Dropping files that are already in the list now skips them and tells you, instead of doing nothing.
Tagging and “Add to Apple Music” are now available for WAV files too.
Adjust the player volume by scrolling over it; the level floats next to the time on hover.
Files whose highs were regenerated by an enhancer are now flagged “Reprocessed” instead of “Bad quality”, so a full-but-faked spectrum doesn't mislead.
Copy a track's generated file name — from your naming pattern — to the clipboard.

title: Copy artwork and tags, drag tracks out
items: Drag a track from the list straight into any external app — Spek, Finder, your player — to check it instantly, no exporting first.
Copy a track's artwork and paste it onto another: from the button on the cover itself or with ⌘C/⌘V, through the system clipboard (to and from other apps too).
Copy a track's full metadata and paste it onto another from the right-click menu — perfect for sharing a release's details across its tracks.
Loudness normalization now actually reaches loud targets: the club preset no longer lands short — it applies the exact gain to target and a true-peak limiter holds the ceiling.

title: Much more reliable spectrum analysis
items: The quality verdict has been rebuilt: dark masters and legitimate 320 kbps MP3s are no longer flagged as fakes for their natural high-end taper.
Grading is now absolute, the way a DJ reads it: reaching ~20 kHz is full quality (320 / lossless), 18–19.5 kHz is suspicious and below 18 kHz is bad — whatever the file's sample rate.
Surco now spots highs regenerated by audio “enhancers”: a track artificially glossed over a low-bitrate source no longer passes as good.
Soft encoder cutoffs (~160–192 kbps) that used to slip through as good quality are now caught and placed where they really are.
Reconstructed-highs detection: files “enhanced” from low-bitrate sources (SBR/enhancers) no longer pass as good quality even when their spectrum reaches the top.

title: Apple Music, always in sync
items: Apple Music sync: re-converting an edited track updates its library copy — tags and artwork — instead of duplicating it.
New “Update in Apple Music” and “Show in Apple Music” actions in the editor; if you deleted the library copy, Surco imports it again.
Click the artwork to view it full size, at its real resolution — embedded art is pulled from the original file, not the thumbnail.
The field menu now also transforms text: Title Case, lowercase or UPPERCASE in one click.
While a track plays, the Dock icon animates with a sound wave.
Editing several tracks at once now shows only the fields you picked in Settings, just like editing one.

title: Built for lots of tracks
items: Your edits never get lost: typing while Surco is still reading a slow file, or while auto-match is querying Discogs, always keeps what you typed.
Much lighter with lots of tracks: covers are kept as thumbnails (converted files still get the full-resolution art) and memory stops growing over long sessions.
Browsing the list feels smoother: BPM, key and loudness analyses wait until you rest on a track, and the one you're looking at always jumps ahead of the ones you left behind.
Every editor field can insert another field's value (artist into composer, title into comment…) from its own menu.
An amber dot marks tracks edited after converting, so you can see at a glance what still needs an update.
The Discogs search remembers what you typed on each track, shows a loading skeleton, and can be retried when it fails.
Names with a leading track number ("104. Artist - Title") now import with the right artist and title.
Adding several tracks to Apple Music shows its progress and can be cancelled; and when saving settings or trashing a file fails, Surco says so instead of failing silently.
Fixed: tracks whose cover was the file's own embedded art converted and landed in Apple Music with no artwork.
The player shows a loading indicator when audio takes a moment to arrive (for example, from a network drive).

title: Richer metadata and quality in three colors
items: New metadata fields: composer, ISRC, mix, original year and a compilation flag. The composer fills itself from the Discogs writing credits.
The quality dot now grades three levels — green, amber and red — so a low-bitrate MP3 dressed up as lossless stands out, with an indicator while the spectrum is analyzed.
A button to clear all of a track's metadata, next to fill-from-filename.
Each list row shows its source format (WAV, FLAC, MP3…) so what still needs converting is obvious at a glance.
Start over in the context menu rebuilds a track from its original file.
The Apple Music check also looks up the Discogs-suggested title, catching library copies saved under the canonical name.

title: BPM, key detection and free forever
items: BPM and key detection: Surco analyzes every track and suggests the tempo and key as one-click chips, in the key notation you prefer.
Surco is now free forever: licensing and limits are gone. If it saves you hours, you can support it with a donation from the Stats tab.
New conversion destination that overwrites the original file, with a confirmation before a batch.
Text search and sorting in the track list, with album-art thumbnails on every row.
The settings folder is configurable: point it at iCloud Drive or Dropbox to share your preferences across Macs.
A shortcut to switch the interface language between English and Spanish on the fly.
A slim progress bar in the header for long background sweeps.

title: A better first run and accessibility throughout
items: The welcome wizard now sets up your output folder, visible and required fields, and teaches you to read the spectrum with a live preview.
New “Apple Music only” destination: the track lands in your library without leaving a copy in the output folder.
Full accessibility pass: the whole app works from the keyboard, plays well with screen readers and meets WCAG AA contrast.
Regenerate a file name in one click using the pattern from Settings.
Open audio files straight from Finder’s “Open With” menu.
New blue spectrogram palette that’s easier to read.

title: Discogs auto-match and rekordbox & Traktor export
items: Auto-match: Surco looks your tracks up on Discogs and applies only high-confidence matches, on import or with a toolbar sweep.
Export your collection as a rekordbox (XML) or Traktor (NML) library.
Batch quality triage: analyze the whole list at once, badge every track with its verdict and filter the suspect rips.
Customizable keyboard shortcuts with clash detection and native menus that update themselves.
Per-track properties panel (codec, bit depth, bitrate, tag formats) and a rename dialog built from metadata chips.
Redesigned icons and tooltips across the app.

title: Star ratings and cover control
items: Rate your tracks with stars; they’re written as tags Traktor, Windows Media Player and foobar2000 understand.
Pick the cover from every image the Discogs release has.
Artwork resolution is always visible and low-quality covers get flagged with a colored dot.
New Discogs release id field in the editor.

title: Loudness and normalization
items: Loudness analysis (LUFS) and optional normalization that preserves your Traktor cues.
Right-click context menu on tracks and search inside the list.
Cancel a batch conversion mid-run.
Configurable default genres applied with one click.
Optionally send the original file to the Trash after converting.

title: Multi-track editing and full albums
items: Multi-selection with bulk editing of shared fields.
Match a whole Discogs release against your files, pairing each track by duration.
Find & replace across every track’s tags, with regular expressions.
Fill tags from the file name in one click.
M4A/AAC, Opus and Ogg Vorbis now accepted as input formats.
The naming pattern supports subfolders to organize the output.

title: Stability
items: More robust importing: hidden files and macOS leftovers are skipped when dropping folders.
Better tag reading on files with embedded pictures.

title: First public release
items: Convert to AIFF, WAV, FLAC or MP3 by dragging your files in.
Discogs tagging with the most likely match suggested.
Spectrum analysis to catch fake lossless before you play out.
Send tracks straight to Apple Music, artwork included.
Template file names, a command palette, track preview and automatic updates.
macOS installs (Intel and Apple Silicon) plus Homebrew.
